preFix.title: Understanding paint finishes for interior walls
preFix.subtitle: What sheen level to choose, and when it matters
preFix.excerpt: A guide to interior paint finishes: the difference between matt, eggshell, satin, and silk emulsions; when to use each; how sheen affects light in a room; and why the finish matters more than the colour when painting a difficult surface.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. City and Guilds Level 2 Painting and Decorating syllabus.

content.text: Paint manufacturers offer every colour in several sheen levels, and the finish affects how the room reads as much as the colour. A deep red in matt reads as muted; the same colour in silk reads as bright. The
runs from flat matt at one end to high gloss at the other. Each step up reflects more light and is more washable; each step down conceals surface imperfections better.

Flat matt has no discernible reflectance. It absorbs light rather than bouncing it, making it the most forgiving finish for walls and ceilings with minor texture, low-spots, or slightly uneven surfaces. The trade-off is washability: a flat matt marks relatively easily and the marks are difficult to clean without leaving a shiny patch. Suited to living rooms, bedrooms, and hallways where the wall is in reasonable condition and takes little direct handling.

Eggshell has a very low sheen (resembling the surface of an eggshell). More washable than flat matt, and slightly more resistant to moisture. Works well in kitchens and hallways where light wiping is needed. Does not conceal surface imperfections as well as matt, so surface preparation matters more.

Satin and silk are near-identical in performance; the name varies by manufacturer. Both have a visible sheen and are easy to wipe clean. Good for children's rooms, kitchens, and utility rooms. The sheen shows every bump in the wall, so the surface needs careful preparation before painting.
New plaster and the mist coat
Any paint system on new plaster starts with a

regardless of the finish chosen. New plaster is alkaline and porous; undiluted paint applied direct beads off the surface or is absorbed unevenly, leaving a patchy finish whatever the sheen. The mist coat seals the surface for the full coat that follows.

A high-sheen paint in a dark colour makes a room feel smaller and more theatrical. The same colour in flat matt reads as softer and more receding. A pale colour in silk reads as brighter and more clinical than the same colour in flat matt. Most paint ranges offer a decorator's matt as a first-time standard and reserve the silk for rooms that need frequent washing.
Worked example: a damp-prone bathroom wall

A bathroom wall prone to condensation needs a moisture-resistant paint, not a standard emulsion. Use a dedicated bathroom emulsion (satin finish, with mould inhibitor) or a solvent-based satin over a suitable primer. Flat matt bathroom paint exists but performs badly here: its porous film absorbs moisture and breaks down quickly.
